subtopic_detail: Routine RhD-negative anti-D prophylaxis schedule
stem: A 26-year-old woman attends her first antenatal visit at 11 weeks in her first pregnancy. Her blood group is O RhD-negative with no atypical antibodies, and her partner is RhD-positive. She is well, with no bleeding or trauma so far in pregnancy. She asks how anti-D immunoglobulin will be given.

data_table.Examination: Uterus not palpable abdominally, no bleeding or bruising
data_table.Investigations: Blood group O RhD-negative, indirect Coombs negative, antibody screen negative
data_table.Medications: Pregnancy multivitamin with iodine and folic acid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate routine schedule for prophylactic anti-D immunoglobulin?

options.text: Routine intramuscular doses at 28 and 34 weeks gestation, plus a postnatal dose if the baby is RhD-positive
options.rationale: Australian RANZCOG guidance is for routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks for RhD-negative women without immune anti-D, plus a postnatal dose within 72 hours if the baby is RhD-positive. (Source: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Defer all anti-D until after delivery and rely on universal postnatal cord blood typing to determine treatment in any future pregnancy
options.rationale: Postnatal-only protocols were superseded because they failed to prevent third-trimester sensitisation; antenatal prophylaxis is now standard in Australia. (Source: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Withhold all routine antenatal prophylaxis and only treat postnatally if the baby is confirmed to be RhD-positive at delivery
options.rationale: Withholding routine antenatal prophylaxis would miss silent feto-maternal haemorrhage in the third trimester and risks sensitisation before delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Monthly intramuscular anti-D injections from 20 weeks gestation through to delivery to ensure ongoing antibody cover for the fetus
options.rationale: Monthly dosing exceeds the evidence base, increases donor exposure and is not recommended in Australia; the program is two routine doses plus event-driven doses. (Source: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: A single intramuscular dose at 28 weeks gestation only if maternal antibody screening remains negative at that visit
options.rationale: A single 28-week dose is not the Australian schedule; the recommended program includes a second routine dose at 34 weeks because anti-D level wanes before delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

explanation.summary: Routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks reduced rates of sensitisation in RhD-negative women to under 0.3 percent. Event-driven anti-D is additionally indicated for sensitising episodes such as bleeding, amniocentesis, external cephalic version or trauma after 12 weeks. Postnatal dose is given within 72 hours when cord blood confirms a RhD-positive baby. Indirect Coombs should be checked at booking and at 28 weeks before prophylaxis to detect pre-existing immune anti-D.
key_points: Two routine antenatal doses at 28 and 34 weeks for RhD-negative women.
Postnatal dose within 72 hours if baby is RhD-positive.
Event-driven dose for sensitising episodes after 12 weeks.
Always recheck indirect Coombs before each routine dose.
Aim is to prevent maternal anti-D formation, not treat existing immunisation.
explanation.pearls: Add a Kleihauer-Betke test if a sensitising event is large (placental abruption, abdominal trauma) - if fetal cells exceed 6 mL, give additional 125 IU anti-D per 1 mL of fetal blood beyond standard dose.

sources.label: RANZCOG Guidelines for the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin
National Blood Authority Australia Anti-D Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Group B streptococcus intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 39+2 weeks is admitted in established labour with regular painful contractions every 3 minutes. Her vaginal-rectal swab at 36 weeks grew Group B Streptococcus. She has no known drug allergies and her previous baby was healthy. Membranes ruptured 90 minutes ago with clear fluid.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9, HR 88, BP 124/76, contractions q3 min
data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm dilated, vertex at -1 station, clear liquor, regular fetal heart at 140
data_table.Investigations: GBS positive on 36-week vaginal-rectal swab, no penicillin allergy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g every 8 hours commenced now and continued throughout labour
options.rationale: Oral regimens do not achieve adequate amniotic fluid levels for intrapartum prophylaxis; the intravenous route is essential for neonatal GBS sepsis prevention. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth
options.rationale: Australian RANZCOG and eTG guidance for known GBS colonisation without penicillin allergy is intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g initial dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 1 g every 12 hours commenced immediately on admission to labour ward
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with clindamycin-resistant isolates; it is not first line when penicillin is tolerated. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin 2 g loading then 1 g every 8 hours from full dilatation onwards
options.rationale: Cefazolin is reserved for low-risk penicillin allergy; in a non-allergic woman benzylpenicillin remains first line and prophylaxis should start in established labour, not full dilatation. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: No antibiotic now and observe with hourly maternal observations only
options.rationale: Withholding prophylaxis in a known carrier in labour misses the window that reduces early-onset neonatal GBS disease by around 80 percent. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

explanation.summary: Universal antenatal screening for GBS by combined vaginal-rectal swab at 35 to 37 weeks is offered in most Australian centres. Positive carriers receive intrapartum intravenous benzylpenicillin to reduce early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis. Adequacy requires the first dose to be given at least 4 hours before delivery where possible. Risk-based protocols use GBS bacteriuria in pregnancy, previous GBS-affected baby, preterm labour, prolonged rupture greater than 18 hours or intrapartum fever as alternative triggers.
key_points: Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV then 1.8 g every 4 hours intrapartum.
Aim for at least 4 hours of antibiotic before birth.
Cefazolin for low-risk penicillin allergy.
Vancomycin or clindamycin only for severe allergy with sensitivity testing.
Risk-based triggers also activate prophylaxis even without swab.
explanation.pearls: GBS bacteriuria at any titre in current pregnancy is itself an indication for intrapartum prophylaxis and the swab can be omitted - treat directly in labour.

subtopic_detail: Combined hormonal contraception eligibility in migraine with aura
stem: A 24-year-old nulliparous woman attends for contraception. She has migraine with visual aura preceding her headaches about twice a month, controlled with rizatriptan. She is a non-smoker, normotensive, and has no other vascular risk factors. She would prefer a method that does not require daily pill-taking.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable, no neurological deficits between attacks
data_table.Investigations: Nil indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate contraceptive recommendation?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing 30 mcg ethinyloestradiol and levonorgestrel
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception is UKMEC 4 (unacceptable health risk) in migraine with aura because of arterial ischaemic stroke risk; cannot be prescribed. (Source: FSRH UKMEC)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare UKMEC

options.text: Combined oral pill but only on cycle days one through twenty-one each month
options.rationale: Cyclical use of a combined pill still exposes the patient to oestrogen and is UKMEC 4 in migraine with aura; the contraindication does not depend on duration of use. (Source: FSRH UKMEC)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare UKMEC

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system as a first-line long-acting reversible method
options.rationale: Migraine with aura is UKMEC category 4 for combined hormonal methods due to ischaemic stroke risk; progestogen-only LARC (LNG-IUS) is category 1 and aligns with her preference for non-daily dosing. (Source: FSRH UKMEC; eTG)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare UKMEC

options.text: Combined contraceptive vaginal ring inserted three weeks of every four-week cycle
options.rationale: The ring contains ethinyloestradiol and is also UKMEC 4 with migraine with aura; route of delivery does not change the oestrogen-driven stroke risk. (Source: FSRH UKMEC)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare UKMEC

options.text: Combined transdermal patch applied weekly with one patch-free week each month
options.rationale: Transdermal combined contraceptive patches are also UKMEC 4 in migraine with aura; oestrogen-containing methods are contraindicated regardless of route. (Source: FSRH UKMEC)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare UKMEC

explanation.summary: Migraine with aura is a marker of increased baseline ischaemic stroke risk; adding combined hormonal contraception multiplies that risk and is classified UKMEC 4 (unacceptable). Migraine without aura at any age is UKMEC 2 for initiation. Acceptable methods in aura include all progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, LNG-IUS, DMPA - DMPA UKMEC 2) and copper IUD (UKMEC 1). Triptan use itself does not change contraception eligibility. Always document the migraine subtype before prescribing.
key_points: Migraine with aura - combined methods UKMEC 4 at any age.
Progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, LNG-IUS) are UKMEC 1 or 2.
Copper IUD UKMEC 1 - non-hormonal alternative.
Aura defined as fully reversible visual, sensory or speech symptoms developing over 5 to 20 minutes.
New aura developing on combined contraception is an absolute indication to stop it immediately.
explanation.pearls: If a woman without aura develops aura while taking a combined pill, stop it the same day and switch to a progestogen-only method - the relative stroke risk rises eight-fold in this scenario.

sources.label: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare UKMEC
Therapeutic Guidelines: Sexual and Reproductive Health

subtopic_detail: Emergency contraception choice within 120 hours of unprotected sex
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents 78 hours after a single episode of unprotected vaginal intercourse on day 14 of a regular 28-day cycle. She is not currently using any contraception and does not wish to be pregnant. She has no contraindications to copper or hormonal contraception and is happy with whichever option is most effective.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable, no pelvic tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative

lead_in: What is the most effective emergency contraception option to offer?

options.text: Yuzpe regimen of high-dose combined oral contraceptive tablets in two divided doses
options.rationale: Yuzpe regimen has high nausea/vomiting rates and inferior efficacy and is reserved for situations where dedicated emergency contraception is unavailable. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare Emergency Contraception

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system inserted today as both emergency and ongoing contraception
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is not currently endorsed as emergency contraception in Australian guidance; the copper IUD remains the only IUD method licensed and recommended for this indication. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare Emergency Contraception

options.text: Copper intrauterine device inserted today and retained for ongoing contraception
options.rationale: Copper IUD is the most effective emergency contraception (failure under 0.1 percent) and may be inserted up to 120 hours after unprotected sex or 5 days after expected ovulation, with the added benefit of ongoing contraception. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare Emergency Contraception

options.text: Oral ulipristal acetate 30 mg taken now as a single dose with food in clinic today
options.rationale: Ulipristal is effective up to 120 hours after intercourse but is less effective than copper IUD overall and does not provide ongoing contraception; a reasonable alternative if she declines IUD. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare Emergency Contraception

options.text: Oral levonorgestrel 1.5 mg taken now as a single dose within the next hour
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel is licensed only up to 72 hours and is less effective than ulipristal or copper IUD; at 78 hours it is outside the licensed window. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare Emergency Contraception

explanation.summary: Three established options exist for emergency contraception in Australia: copper IUD (up to 120 hours, most effective, ongoing contraception), oral ulipristal acetate (up to 120 hours), and oral levonorgestrel (up to 72 hours). Pregnancy testing should be negative at presentation. Counsel that ulipristal interferes with progestogen-based contraception for 5 days, so hormonal contraception should be delayed accordingly. Repeat pregnancy test in 3 weeks if next period is delayed or lighter than expected.
key_points: Copper IUD up to 120 hours - most effective.
Ulipristal acetate 30 mg up to 120 hours - oral first-line.
Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg up to 72 hours only.
Ulipristal delays restart of progestogen contraception by 5 days.
Repeat pregnancy test in 3 weeks if delayed menses.
explanation.pearls: If BMI is over 26, double the levonorgestrel dose to 3 mg or use ulipristal or copper IUD instead - standard 1.5 mg levonorgestrel is markedly less effective at higher BMI.

sources.label: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare Emergency Contraception
Therapeutic Guidelines: Sexual and Reproductive Health

subtopic_detail: Recurrent miscarriage workup after three consecutive losses
stem: A 33-year-old woman presents for review after her third consecutive first-trimester miscarriage. Her three losses all occurred between 8 and 11 weeks gestation with no live births. She is otherwise well, with regular menstrual cycles and no medical history of note. She and her partner are non-consanguineous.

data_table.Examination: Normal general and pelvic examination
data_table.Investigations: Routine antenatal bloods normal in last pregnancy; not yet investigated for cause

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial investigation panel?

options.text: Antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotype, pelvic ultrasound and thyroid function
options.rationale: Australian and RCOG guidance recommend offering antiphospholipid screen (lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin, anti-beta2 GPI), parental karyotyping, pelvic imaging for uterine anatomy and thyroid function after three consecutive losses. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Day 21 progesterone with serial luteinising hormone tracking across two ovulatory cycles
options.rationale: Luteal phase defect testing is not validated and luteal phase support has unclear benefit in unselected recurrent miscarriage. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Endometrial biopsy for chronic endometritis and infectious screen including TORCH panel
options.rationale: Endometrial biopsy and TORCH panel are not routine first-line investigations; chronic endometritis is emerging research but not standard. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Routine factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene and MTHFR thrombophilia screen as initial workup
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilia screening is not recommended for recurrent first-trimester loss outside research settings; only antiphospholipid syndrome screening is supported. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Repeat saline-infusion sonohysterography only after a fourth consecutive pregnancy loss occurs
options.rationale: Waiting until a fourth loss delays diagnosis; uterine cavity assessment is part of the initial workup at three consecutive losses. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage)

explanation.summary: Recurrent miscarriage is defined as three or more consecutive losses in Australian guidance (some bodies now use two). Identifiable causes include antiphospholipid syndrome (5 to 15 percent), parental chromosomal rearrangement (3 to 5 percent), uterine anomalies (10 to 15 percent on imaging), uncontrolled thyroid disease and diabetes. About 50 percent remain unexplained. Treatment is cause-directed - aspirin plus LMWH for confirmed APS doubles live-birth rates. Routine inherited thrombophilia screening, immune therapies and progesterone for unexplained loss are not supported.
key_points: Define as 3 (or 2 per some guidelines) consecutive pregnancy losses.
First-line tests: APS screen, karyotype both partners, pelvic USS, TFT.
APS treatment: low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH from positive pregnancy test.
Inherited thrombophilia screen not routinely indicated.
Around 50 percent remain unexplained with good prognosis on supportive care.
explanation.pearls: Antiphospholipid antibodies must be positive on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart to confirm APS - a single result is not sufficient before committing to long-term aspirin plus LMWH.

subtopic_detail: Initial female subfertility investigation pathway
stem: A 30-year-old woman attends with her partner after 14 months of regular unprotected intercourse without conception. Her cycles are regular at 28 to 30 days with no intermenstrual bleeding or significant dysmenorrhoea. Her partner has fathered a child in a previous relationship 4 years ago and is well.

data_table.Examination: Normal abdominal and pelvic examination, no thyroid or hirsutism findings
data_table.Investigations: No fertility investigations performed yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line female investigation?

options.text: Mid-luteal day 21 progesterone level taken in a typical 28-day cycle to confirm ovulation
options.rationale: Australian primary care guidance recommends mid-luteal progesterone (about 7 days before next expected period) to confirm ovulation as the first-line test in regular cycles, alongside semen analysis for the male partner. (Source: RACGP Fertility)

options.text: Postcoital cervical mucus test taken in clinic 6 to 12 hours after intercourse at midcycle
options.rationale: Postcoital testing has poor reproducibility and is no longer recommended in mainstream subfertility workup. (Source: RACGP Fertility)

options.text: Anti-mullerian hormone level for ovarian reserve assessment as the initial fertility investigation
options.rationale: AMH is useful for predicting IVF response and timing decisions but does not predict natural fertility and is not first-line in a woman under 35 with regular cycles. (Source: RACGP Fertility)

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy and dye to assess for pelvic adhesions and unsuspected endometriosis
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is reserved for suspected endometriosis or after non-invasive tests; it is not an initial investigation in unexplained subfertility. (Source: RACGP Fertility)

options.text: Hysterosalpingo-contrast sonography of the uterine cavity and fallopian tubes as initial test
options.rationale: Tubal patency testing is second-line, indicated when basic ovulation and semen tests are normal or when pelvic infection or endometriosis is suspected. (Source: RACGP Fertility)

explanation.summary: Subfertility is defined as failure to conceive after 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse (6 months if the woman is 35 or older). Australian initial workup includes mid-luteal progesterone for ovulation, TSH, prolactin, FBE, pelvic USS for anatomy and AFC, plus semen analysis for the partner. Tubal patency testing (HyCoSy or HSG) is added if these are normal. Time to referral to a fertility specialist is shorter (6 months) if the woman is 35 or older, has known anovulation, tubal disease, endometriosis or significant male factor.
key_points: Define subfertility: 12 months (or 6 months if woman 35+).
First-line female: mid-luteal progesterone, TSH, prolactin, pelvic USS.
First-line male: semen analysis (WHO 2021 criteria).
AMH guides IVF planning, not natural fertility prognosis.
Early specialist referral if known tubal, endometriosis or male factor.
explanation.pearls: Mid-luteal progesterone greater than 30 nmol/L confirms ovulation; persistently low results despite regular cycles suggest luteal-phase deficiency or anovulatory bleeds, prompting cycle tracking with serial USS.

subtopic_detail: PCOS management in a woman not seeking pregnancy
stem: A 23-year-old woman presents with cycles every 45 to 80 days, facial and chin hirsutism, and 6 kg weight gain over 12 months. She is not currently sexually active and does not wish to conceive in the next several years. Pelvic ultrasound shows polycystic ovarian morphology bilaterally and biochemistry confirms hyperandrogenism with normal thyroid and prolactin.

data_table.Examination: Modified Ferriman-Gallwey score 12, no acanthosis nigricans, no virilisation
data_table.Investigations: Total testosterone 3.4 nmol/L (elevated), free androgen index elevated, TSH normal, prolactin normal, fasting glucose 5.4, HbA1c 5.6

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill alongside lifestyle and weight-management advice
options.rationale: International evidence-based PCOS guideline first-line for cycle regulation, hyperandrogenism and contraception is a combined oral contraceptive, in combination with lifestyle modification. (Source: International Evidence-based PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Metformin 500 mg twice daily titrated up to 1500 mg as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Metformin is recommended as an adjunct or first-line if metabolic features predominate, but COCP outperforms metformin for hirsutism and menstrual regulation. (Source: International Evidence-based PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Spironolactone 100 mg daily monotherapy without reliable contraception in place
options.rationale: Anti-androgens require effective contraception because of risk of feminisation of a male fetus; should follow or accompany COCP, not precede it. (Source: International Evidence-based PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Clomiphene citrate for cycle regulation taken on cycle days two through six monthly
options.rationale: Clomiphene is an ovulation induction agent for women seeking conception, not for cycle regulation in a woman avoiding pregnancy. (Source: International Evidence-based PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Cyclical progestogen withdrawal alone every 90 days to induce a regular monthly bleed
options.rationale: Cyclical progestogen protects the endometrium but does not address hirsutism, contraception or cardiovascular risk; less complete than COCP for this patient. (Source: International Evidence-based PCOS Guideline)

explanation.summary: PCOS is diagnosed by Rotterdam criteria (any 2 of oligo-anovulation, hyperandrogenism clinical or biochemical, polycystic morphology) after excluding mimics. Management is goal-directed: COCP for cycle regulation, hirsutism and contraception; metformin if metabolic features dominate or in addition; anti-androgens (spironolactone, cyproterone) for hirsutism on top of reliable contraception. Lifestyle change is foundational - even 5 percent weight loss improves cycle regularity and metabolic profile. Annual screening for impaired glucose tolerance and mental health.
key_points: Rotterdam: any 2 of oligo-anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic morphology.
COCP first-line if not pregnancy-seeking.
Metformin if metabolic features predominate or alongside COCP.
Spironolactone for hirsutism only with reliable contraception.
Lifestyle first - 5 percent weight loss restores cycles in many.
explanation.pearls: Endometrial cancer risk in PCOS rises sharply if menses occur fewer than every 3 months; either COCP or cyclical progestogen for endometrial protection is mandatory long-term.

subtopic_detail: Australian cervical screening program HPV-based primary screening
stem: A 26-year-old woman attends for her first cervical screening test. She is sexually active with two lifetime partners and has had HPV vaccination as a teenager. She has never had any abnormal screening result and is in a stable relationship. She has no abnormal bleeding or discharge.

data_table.Examination: Normal pelvic and speculum examination, cervix appears healthy
data_table.Investigations: First cervical screening test today

lead_in: What is the most appropriate cervical screening test and follow-up interval?

options.text: HPV-based screening only after the next pregnancy or after age 30 years if no pregnancy occurs
options.rationale: Screening starts at age 25 regardless of pregnancy status; deferring to age 30 misses preventable high-grade abnormalities. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: HPV-based primary screening test, repeated every 5 years if oncogenic HPV is not detected
options.rationale: The National Cervical Screening Program uses HPV-based primary screening from age 25 to 74 every 5 years if oncogenic HPV is not detected, with reflex liquid-based cytology when HPV positive. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Conventional Pap cytology, repeated every 2 years if cytology shows no intraepithelial lesion
options.rationale: Australia replaced 2-yearly Pap cytology with 5-yearly HPV primary testing in 2017 because HPV testing has superior sensitivity for high-grade lesions. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: HPV-based primary screening test, repeated every 3 years irrespective of the result obtained
options.rationale: Three-yearly screening is recommended for women who are HPV positive non-16/18 with low-grade cytology under the surveillance pathway, not as primary interval. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Colposcopy and biopsy at the first visit to baseline this woman with prior HPV exposure
options.rationale: Colposcopy is not a screening test; it is indicated only for HPV 16/18 detection, persistent HPV other-types with abnormal cytology, or symptoms. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The renewed Australian National Cervical Screening Program from December 2017 uses HPV-based primary testing for women 25 to 74 years every 5 years if oncogenic HPV is not detected. HPV 16/18 detection prompts direct colposcopy. HPV non-16/18 detection prompts reflex cytology - high-grade cytology to colposcopy, low-grade or normal cytology to 12-month repeat HPV. Self-collection is offered universally as an option since July 2022. Exit testing at 70 to 74 ends screening if HPV-negative.
key_points: Routine screen: HPV-based, age 25 to 74, every 5 years.
HPV 16/18 detected: refer for colposcopy.
HPV non-16/18: reflex cytology; high-grade triages to colposcopy.
Self-collection now universally available.
Vaccinated women still need screening - vaccine does not cover all oncogenic types.
explanation.pearls: Self-collected vaginal swabs have equivalent sensitivity to clinician-collected samples for HPV detection - offer them to women who decline speculum examination including survivors of trauma.

subtopic_detail: Endometrial hyperplasia with atypia management
stem: A 56-year-old postmenopausal woman with a BMI of 34 presents with three episodes of vaginal bleeding over the past 4 months. Transvaginal ultrasound shows endometrial thickness of 14 mm with heterogeneous echotexture. Hysteroscopy and biopsy report endometrial hyperplasia with atypia. She has completed her family and has no comorbidities prohibiting surgery.

data_table.Examination: Atrophic vagina, small uterus, no palpable adnexal mass
data_table.Investigations: Endometrial thickness 14 mm; biopsy: complex hyperplasia with atypia

lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?

options.text: Total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as the recommended treatment for this lesion
options.rationale: Endometrial hyperplasia with atypia carries a 25 to 40 percent risk of concurrent endometrial carcinoma and 28 percent progression risk; total hysterectomy with BSO is first-line in postmenopausal women. (Source: RANZCOG Endometrial Hyperplasia)

options.text: Oral medroxyprogesterone acetate 20 mg daily with surveillance biopsies every 6 months
options.rationale: Oral high-dose progestogens are an alternative when surgery is contraindicated; LNG-IUS gives more consistent endometrial exposure when medical therapy is required. (Source: RANZCOG Endometrial Hyperplasia)

options.text: Repeat endometrial biopsy in 3 months without intervening treatment to confirm persistence
options.rationale: Delayed treatment of confirmed atypical hyperplasia risks progression to invasive carcinoma; biopsy already confirms the lesion. (Source: RANZCOG Endometrial Hyperplasia)

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system in situ as long-term endometrial suppressive therapy
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is reserved for atypical hyperplasia in fertility-sparing or unfit-for-surgery women with 6-monthly endometrial biopsies; not first-line in postmenopausal women fit for surgery. (Source: RANZCOG Endometrial Hyperplasia)

options.text: Endometrial ablation under hysteroscopic vision with annual follow-up biopsy thereafter
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is contraindicated in atypical hyperplasia because residual endometrial tissue can harbour or develop carcinoma that is then occult on subsequent imaging. (Source: RANZCOG Endometrial Hyperplasia)

explanation.summary: Endometrial hyperplasia is divided into hyperplasia without atypia (1 to 3 percent progression) and atypical hyperplasia (28 percent progression and high rate of co-existent endometrioid adenocarcinoma). Postmenopausal women with atypia are offered total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Premenopausal women wishing fertility receive LNG-IUS with 3 to 6 monthly biopsies until two consecutive negative samples. Risk factors include unopposed oestrogen, tamoxifen, PCOS, Lynch syndrome, obesity and chronic anovulation.
key_points: Atypia: 25 to 40 percent concurrent carcinoma risk; hysterectomy + BSO if postmenopausal.
No atypia: progestogen treatment (oral or LNG-IUS) and surveillance.
LNG-IUS preferred fertility-sparing option for atypia.
Endometrial ablation contraindicated in atypia.
Manage obesity and oestrogen exposure to prevent recurrence.
explanation.pearls: Lynch syndrome screening (Amsterdam criteria or immunohistochemistry on the hysterectomy specimen for MMR proteins) is indicated for all atypical hyperplasia cases under age 50.

subtopic_detail: Symptomatic uterine fibroid first-line medical therapy
stem: A 38-year-old G2P2 woman with heavy menstrual bleeding, cycle length 28 days, and ferritin 12 mcg/L attends for management. Pelvic ultrasound shows three intramural fibroids each under 4 cm with normal cavity contour and a uterus equivalent to 10-week size. She has completed her family but does not wish to undergo a major surgical procedure. She has no contraindications to hormonal therapy.

data_table.Examination: Bulky uterus equivalent to 10 weeks; no adnexal mass
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L, ferritin 12 mcg/L, fibroid total volume small, cavity normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line medical management?

options.text: Uterine artery embolisation performed by interventional radiology as the first-line uterus-conserving option
options.rationale: UAE is a uterus-conserving procedural option after failed or unsuitable medical therapy; medical management with LNG-IUS is offered first when appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill in extended-cycle regimen with quarterly hormone-free intervals
options.rationale: Combined oral contraceptive reduces bleeding modestly compared with LNG-IUS and exposes the patient to oestrogen; second-line in this scenario. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Hysterectomy as the definitive curative procedure for symptomatic intramural fibroids in this case
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is definitive but the patient explicitly wishes to avoid major surgery; medical therapy should be tried first when feasible. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system after exclusion of intracavitary distortion of the cavity
options.rationale: RANZCOG and RACGP first-line medical therapy for heavy menstrual bleeding from intramural fibroids with normal cavity is LNG-IUS, with reduction in bleeding of around 80 percent at 6 months. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Gonadotrophin-releasing hormone agonist depot for 6 months alongside menopausal-symptom add-back therapy
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are used short-term pre-operatively to shrink fibroids or in selected cases; not first-line long-term medical therapy. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

explanation.summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding from small intramural or subserosal fibroids with a normal cavity responds well to LNG-IUS (Mirena). Submucosal fibroids (FIGO type 0, 1, 2) distort the cavity and may displace the device - hysteroscopic myomectomy is preferred. Tranexamic acid and NSAIDs are useful adjuncts on bleeding days. GnRH agonists with add-back are reserved for pre-operative shrinkage or perimenopausal bridging. UAE, focused ultrasound, myomectomy and hysterectomy are procedural options matched to fertility wishes and symptom severity.
key_points: Intramural fibroid + normal cavity: LNG-IUS first.
Submucosal fibroid: hysteroscopic myomectomy.
Tranexamic acid and NSAIDs on bleeding days as adjuncts.
GnRH agonist - short-term pre-op or perimenopausal bridging.
Hysterectomy is definitive if family complete and other measures fail.
explanation.pearls: A submucosal fibroid greater than 50 percent intramural (FIGO type 2) often expels an LNG-IUS within 3 months - check cavity contour with saline-infusion sonohysterography before insertion if uncertain.

subtopic_detail: Hyperemesis gravidarum admission and inpatient management
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 9 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department with 5 days of relentless vomiting more than 10 times per day. She has lost 5 percent of her pre-pregnancy weight and cannot tolerate oral fluids. Urinalysis shows 3+ ketones. Her PUQE-24 score is 14. She is dizzy on standing and has not slept overnight due to vomiting.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58 supine to 84/52 standing, HR 108, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor, no abdominal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Urine ketones 3+, Na 134, K 3.2, urea 6.8, creatinine 88, TSH 0.4, beta-hCG 56000
data_table.Medications: Pyridoxine and doxylamine tried at home with no benefit

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial inpatient management bundle?

options.text: Discharge with oral pyridoxine-doxylamine titration and outpatient ketone monitoring by GP daily
options.rationale: Discharge is inappropriate with significant ketonuria, postural drop and failed first-line oral therapy; she needs IV rehydration and parenteral antiemetic. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy

options.text: Admit with IV thiamine, IV normal saline with potassium, and ondansetron orally dispersible or intravenous
options.rationale: PUQE 13 or more with dehydration and ketonuria warrants admission; thiamine before glucose-containing fluids prevents Wernicke encephalopathy; ondansetron is second-line antiemetic when pyridoxine and doxylamine fail in Australian guidance. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy

options.text: Admit and commence IV 5 percent dextrose first to correct presumed hypoglycaemia before any other agent
options.rationale: Giving dextrose before thiamine in a malnourished pregnant woman risks precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy; thiamine must come first. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy

options.text: Admit with IV corticosteroid pulse (methylprednisolone) as the first-line antiemetic for refractory cases
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are reserved for refractory hyperemesis after failure of conventional antiemetics; not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy

options.text: Admit with oral metoclopramide as the initial antiemetic and oral hydration trial over six hours
options.rationale: Oral therapy is inappropriate with frequent vomiting and dehydration; IV antiemetics and IV fluids are required initially. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is defined by intractable nausea/vomiting with weight loss greater than 5 percent, dehydration and electrolyte disturbance, typically peaking 8 to 12 weeks. PUQE-24 stratifies severity. First-line outpatient is pyridoxine plus doxylamine; second-line metoclopramide, ondansetron or prochlorperazine. Admission criteria include failure of oral antiemetics, ketonuria 2+ or more, weight loss greater than 5 percent and inability to tolerate oral fluids. Always give thiamine 100 mg IV before glucose-containing fluids to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.
key_points: PUQE-24 score guides outpatient versus inpatient.
Thiamine before dextrose to prevent Wernicke.
First-line oral: pyridoxine + doxylamine.
Second-line: metoclopramide, ondansetron, prochlorperazine.
Refractory: corticosteroids or admission to nutritional support.
explanation.pearls: Suspect molar pregnancy when nausea is severe and beta-hCG is markedly elevated (over 200000 IU/L) without a viable singleton on ultrasound - send a TVUS even when nausea seems to explain the picture.

sources.label: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy
Therapeutic Guidelines: Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy delivery timing by bile acid
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 35+0 weeks reports relentless palmar and plantar itching, worse at night, for the past 10 days with no rash. Her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated. CTG today is reactive and growth scan a week ago showed normal interval growth and Doppler. She is otherwise well with no jaundice.

data_table.Examination: Excoriations on palms and soles, no jaundice, soft abdomen, normal fundal height
data_table.Investigations: Total bile acids 108 micromol/L, ALT 96 U/L, bilirubin 14 micromol/L, normal viral hepatitis screen

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan for delivery timing?

options.text: Continue ursodeoxycholic acid and plan elective caesarean section at 39 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Cholestasis itself is not an indication for caesarean, and 39 weeks is too late given the bile-acid threshold; vaginal birth at 36 weeks is appropriate. (Source: RCOG Obstetric Cholestasis Green-top)

options.text: Await spontaneous labour with twice-weekly CTG and growth scans every two weeks
options.rationale: CTG and Doppler do not reliably predict stillbirth in cholestasis; reassuring surveillance does not justify expectant management when bile acids exceed 100 micromol/L. (Source: RCOG Obstetric Cholestasis Green-top)

options.text: Discharge home for outpatient weekly bile-acid trend with admission only if levels exceed 200
options.rationale: Outpatient temporising at this bile-acid level delays delivery beyond the 35 to 36 week stillbirth-risk inflection point. (Source: RCOG Obstetric Cholestasis Green-top)

options.text: Plan induction of labour at 36 weeks given bile acids over 100 micromol/L threshold
options.rationale: Total bile acids 100 micromol/L or more substantially elevate the risk of stillbirth from around 35 weeks; RANZCOG and RCOG support induction at 36 weeks to minimise that risk. (Source: RCOG Obstetric Cholestasis Green-top)

options.text: Increase ursodeoxycholic acid to maximum dose and reassess bile acids in 7 days time
options.rationale: Ursodeoxycholic acid improves maternal pruritus but no major trial has shown reduced stillbirth at high bile-acid levels; deferring delivery for medical optimisation is not supported. (Source: RCOG Obstetric Cholestasis Green-top)

explanation.summary: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy typically presents in the third trimester with itch (often palms/soles) and raised bile acids without a rash. Stillbirth risk rises sharply once total bile acids reach 100 micromol/L or more. Pruritus is managed symptomatically with ursodeoxycholic acid (improves itch and biochemistry but not stillbirth at high levels). Delivery timing is bile-acid driven: under 40 micromol/L delivery by 39 weeks; 40 to 99 deliver by 38 to 39 weeks; 100 or more deliver by 35 to 36 weeks. Symptoms resolve within days of birth; recurrence risk in subsequent pregnancy is 60 to 90 percent.
key_points: Bile acids 100+ - deliver at 35 to 36 weeks.
Bile acids 40 to 99 - deliver by 38 to 39 weeks.
Ursodeoxycholic acid improves itch but not stillbirth at high bile-acid levels.
CTG and Doppler do not predict stillbirth in cholestasis.
Recurrence risk 60 to 90 percent in subsequent pregnancies.
explanation.pearls: Postnatal recovery of bile acids and ALT should occur within 4 to 6 weeks; persistent abnormalities warrant hepatology referral for chronic cholestatic liver disease and oestrogen-containing contraception should be deferred until biochemistry normalises.

subtopic_detail: Significant varicella exposure in non-immune pregnant woman
stem: A 28-year-old woman at 27 weeks gestation in her first pregnancy presents 36 hours after caring for her 4-year-old nephew who developed a vesicular varicella rash during the visit. She has no prior history of chickenpox or vaccination and is uncertain whether she had the disease as a child. She has no rash herself and is feeling well. She has no chronic medical conditions.

data_table.Examination: No rash on skin or mucosa; no respiratory symptoms; uterus appropriate for gestation
data_table.Investigations: Varicella zoster IgG sent today; result pending. No PCR samples available yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence prophylactic oral aciclovir today and continue to 14 days post-exposure
options.rationale: Oral aciclovir prophylaxis is reserved for situations where VZIG is unavailable or after 96 hours; VZIG within 96 hours remains the preferred first-line. (Source: RANZCOG Varicella in Pregnancy)

options.text: Reassure as antenatal varicella exposure rarely affects the fetus and require no specific action
options.rationale: Maternal chickenpox in pregnancy carries 1 to 2 percent fetal varicella syndrome before 28 weeks and severe maternal morbidity from varicella pneumonitis; reassurance alone is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG Varicella in Pregnancy)

options.text: Give live attenuated varicella vaccine today to provide rapid post-exposure protection
options.rationale: Live varicella vaccine is contraindicated in pregnancy; it cannot be used for post-exposure prophylaxis in pregnant women. (Source: RANZCOG Varicella in Pregnancy)

options.text: Admit and commence intravenous aciclovir empirically until IgG confirms her immune status
options.rationale: IV aciclovir is reserved for established varicella complicated by pneumonitis or severe disease, not asymptomatic post-exposure prophylaxis. (Source: RANZCOG Varicella in Pregnancy)

options.text: Await urgent varicella IgG result, then give VZIG within 96 hours if IgG is negative
options.rationale: Australian guidance is to confirm non-immunity by urgent VZV IgG and administer VZIG within 96 hours of exposure if negative, to reduce maternal severity and fetal infection risk. (Source: RANZCOG Varicella in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Significant varicella exposure in pregnancy (household, indoors more than 5 minutes face to face, or hospital ward contact) requires confirmation of maternal immunity. If IgG-negative, give VZIG within 96 hours (up to 10 days is licensed in some sources) to attenuate maternal disease and reduce fetal infection. If chickenpox subsequently develops, oral aciclovir within 24 hours of rash onset is offered if more than 20 weeks. IV aciclovir for any pneumonitis, neurological involvement or severe disease. Maternal exposure within 7 days of delivery requires neonatal VZIG.
key_points: Confirm immunity with VZV IgG urgently after exposure.
Non-immune - VZIG within 96 hours of exposure.
If rash develops over 20 weeks - oral aciclovir within 24 hours.
Severe maternal disease or pneumonitis - IV aciclovir.
Vaccinate postnatally before discharge if non-immune.
explanation.pearls: Fetal varicella syndrome (skin scarring along dermatomes, limb hypoplasia, chorioretinitis) occurs only with maternal infection before 20 weeks; later infection risks neonatal disease if maternal rash develops 7 days before to 7 days after delivery.

subtopic_detail: Postnatal hypertension antihypertensive choice in breastfeeding mother
stem: A 32-year-old woman who developed pre-eclampsia at 36 weeks delivered 6 days ago after a successful induction. She has been on oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily but reports persistent fatigue and slow heart rate. Today her clinic BP is 152/98 with no proteinuria, headache, or visual disturbance. She is exclusively breastfeeding her newborn.

data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes, no clonus, no peripheral oedema, lochia normal
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 198, LFT normal, creatinine 78, urine PCR 18
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg PO three times daily, paracetamol PRN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antihypertensive change?

options.text: Switch to oral methyldopa 250 mg three times daily to safely cover the breastfeeding period
options.rationale: Methyldopa is a pregnancy agent with sedative and depressive effects; it is generally avoided postnatally because better-tolerated options are available. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Switch to oral atenolol 25 mg daily for ease of dosing and convenience for the patient
options.rationale: Atenolol is concentrated in breast milk and is generally avoided in breastfeeding mothers due to risk of neonatal bradycardia and hypoglycaemia. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Continue current labetalol dose unchanged and recheck blood pressure in 1 week clinic
options.rationale: BP 152/98 with bradycardia at 6 days postpartum needs intervention now; delaying review for a week risks severe hypertension and cardiovascular events. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Switch labetalol to enalapril 5 mg daily titrated to blood pressure target
options.rationale: Enalapril is the postnatal antihypertensive of choice when labetalol is poorly tolerated or BP remains elevated; it is compatible with breastfeeding and has good cardiovascular evidence. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Add nifedipine modified-release 30 mg daily to current labetalol as combination therapy
options.rationale: Nifedipine add-on is reasonable, but the woman is bradycardic on labetalol; switching to enalapril removes the symptomatic side effect rather than compounding it. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Postnatal hypertension follows pre-eclampsia in around 30 percent of cases and can peak at days 3 to 6. Target BP under 140/90 (or under 135/85 if comorbid). Breastfeeding-compatible agents include enalapril and captopril (preferred), labetalol, nifedipine MR, and metoprolol. Avoid methyldopa (postnatal depression risk), atenolol (concentrates in milk), ARBs in newborn period and diuretics during established breastfeeding. Review BP weekly until controlled and at 6 weeks postnatal; refer to cardiovascular health review by 6 months given doubled lifetime CV risk.
key_points: Target BP under 140/90 postnatal.
First-line breastfeeding: enalapril or captopril.
Second-line: labetalol, nifedipine MR, metoprolol.
Avoid: methyldopa postnatal, atenolol in breastfeeding, ARBs.
Lifetime CV risk doubled - refer for 6-month review.
explanation.pearls: Pre-eclampsia predicts a doubling of lifetime cardiovascular and stroke risk - schedule a 6-month structured cardiovascular review with lipids, glucose and BMI rather than treating it as a one-off pregnancy event.

subtopic_detail: Maternal pulmonary hypertension preconception counselling
stem: A 29-year-old woman with idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension on macitentan and tadalafil attends preconception clinic to discuss starting a family with her partner. Her NYHA functional class is II and right heart catheter at the last review showed mean PAP 38 mmHg with preserved RV function. She is currently using condoms only and asks about pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68, HR 84, SpO2 96 percent on room air, BMI 25
data_table.Examination: RV heave, loud P2, no peripheral oedema, NYHA II
data_table.Investigations: Echo PASP 60 mmHg, RV function preserved, six minute walk 380 m
data_table.Medications: Macitentan 10 mg daily, tadalafil 40 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling and management plan?

options.text: Counsel that pregnancy is contraindicated and offer effective contraception with a long-acting method
options.rationale: Pulmonary arterial hypertension is WHO class IV (pregnancy contraindicated; maternal mortality up to 30 percent); LARC (LNG-IUS) is the preferred contraceptive in modified WHO 4 cardiac disease. (Source: RANZCOG Cardiac Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Recommend assisted reproduction with donor egg and surrogacy as her preferred path to parenthood
options.rationale: Surrogacy may be discussed but only after frank discussion of contraindication; first step is to offer effective contraception and refer to a high-risk team. (Source: RANZCOG Cardiac Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Counsel that pregnancy is acceptable provided delivery is planned by elective caesarean section
options.rationale: Caesarean does not alter the maternal mortality from peripartum cardiac decompensation and pulmonary hypertensive crisis; mode of delivery does not change the underlying contraindication. (Source: RANZCOG Cardiac Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan pregnancy with cessation of pulmonary vasodilators and serial echocardiography monitoring
options.rationale: Endothelin receptor antagonists are teratogenic but ceasing all pulmonary vasodilators destabilises maternal physiology; pregnancy itself remains contraindicated regardless of medication adjustment. (Source: RANZCOG Cardiac Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan pregnancy and continue current PAH therapy unchanged through the entire pregnancy course
options.rationale: Macitentan is an endothelin receptor antagonist with severe teratogenicity (limb and craniofacial defects); cannot continue without contraception. (Source: RANZCOG Cardiac Disease in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: The modified WHO classification of maternal cardiovascular risk classes pulmonary arterial hypertension, severe systemic ventricular dysfunction (EF under 30 percent or NYHA III/IV), severe mitral or aortic stenosis, severe coarctation, Marfan with aortic root over 45 mm and bicuspid valve with aortic root over 50 mm as class IV - pregnancy contraindicated. Counselling should be non-judgemental but explicit and accompanied by effective contraception, ideally LNG-IUS. If pregnancy occurs, urgent referral to a tertiary cardio-obstetric service for early termination discussion or coordinated specialised care.
key_points: Modified WHO IV cardiac disease - pregnancy contraindicated.
Pulmonary arterial hypertension carries up to 30 percent maternal mortality.
Effective contraception, ideally LNG-IUS, is essential.
Macitentan and ambrisentan are major teratogens.
Refer accidental pregnancy urgently to tertiary cardio-obstetric service.
explanation.pearls: Insertion of an LNG-IUS in a patient with significant pulmonary hypertension should be performed in a monitored setting because vasovagal response to insertion can precipitate pulmonary hypertensive crisis - have IV access and anaesthetic backup available.

sources.label: ESC Cardiovascular Disease during Pregnancy Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Obstetric anal sphincter injury repair and antibiotic prophylaxis
stem: A 25-year-old primigravida has just delivered a 3850 g infant after a 14-minute second stage. On systematic perineal examination by the registrar, you identify a tear extending through the perineal body, the external anal sphincter (more than 50 percent of its thickness) and into the internal anal sphincter, with intact rectal mucosa. Bleeding is controlled with pressure. She is comfortable with her epidural still functioning.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72, HR 86, T 37.0, blood loss 350 mL
data_table.Examination: 3c tear by Sultan classification; rectal mucosa intact; sphincter ends retracted
data_table.Investigations: Hb 110 g/L on FBC done after delivery

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Pack the wound with gauze and arrange repair in the morning by the rostered consultant
options.rationale: Delaying repair beyond 12 hours increases infection risk and impairs healing; repair should occur as soon as conditions allow. (Source: RANZCOG Perineal Trauma)

options.text: Apply absorbable sutures to skin only and arrange outpatient anorectal physiotherapy review
options.rationale: Sphincter ends must be identified and reapproximated; closing skin only without sphincter repair leaves anal incontinence risk extremely high. (Source: RANZCOG Perineal Trauma)

options.text: Transfer to theatre for repair under regional anaesthesia with IV antibiotic cover
options.rationale: Third-degree tears require repair in theatre with adequate lighting, regional or general anaesthesia and IV broad-spectrum antibiotics; primary repair on labour bed risks poor outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG Perineal Trauma)

options.text: Repair on the labour bed under top-up epidural with absorbable monofilament suture
options.rationale: Bedside repair of OASIS is associated with poorer functional outcomes; theatre conditions with surgical assistance are required. (Source: RANZCOG Perineal Trauma)

options.text: Refer for primary defunctioning colostomy as the safest initial surgical management option
options.rationale: Defunctioning stoma is not used routinely for OASIS; primary repair in theatre with antibiotics is standard, with stoma reserved for fourth-degree tears with rectal involvement and breakdown. (Source: RANZCOG Perineal Trauma)

explanation.summary: OASIS occurs in around 2 to 3 percent of vaginal births. Sultan classification: 3a less than 50 percent EAS thickness; 3b more than 50 percent EAS thickness; 3c full thickness EAS with IAS involvement; 4 includes rectal mucosa. Repair in theatre with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (typically amoxicillin-clavulanate or cefazolin plus metronidazole) by an experienced operator. Postoperative analgesia, laxatives for 10 to 14 days, dedicated physiotherapy and follow-up at 6 to 12 weeks. Future delivery: counselling about elective caesarean if symptomatic, vaginal birth otherwise reasonable.
key_points: Sultan 3c: more than 50 percent EAS + IAS involvement.
Theatre repair with regional or general anaesthesia.
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics at repair and oral course postnatal.
Postoperative laxatives 10 to 14 days, no NSAIDs after 4th degree.
Follow-up at 6 to 12 weeks with symptom review and physiotherapy.
explanation.pearls: Endoanal ultrasound at 12 weeks postnatal is the gold-standard assessment of repair integrity and predicts the success of subsequent vaginal delivery; offer it to all OASIS patients planning further pregnancies.

sources.label: RCOG Third- and Fourth-degree Perineal Tears Green-top

subtopic_detail: Twin-twin transfusion syndrome diagnosis and referral
stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 with a monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 19 weeks is reviewed in maternal-fetal medicine. Her surveillance scan shows twin 1 with deepest vertical pocket 1 cm and twin 2 with deepest vertical pocket 9 cm; bladders of both fetuses are visible and Doppler studies are normal. Both fetuses are appropriately grown. The couple ask about management options.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height 22 cm
data_table.Investigations: MCDA twins; DVP twin 1 = 1 cm; DVP twin 2 = 9 cm; both bladders seen; normal Dopplers

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Expectant management with twice-weekly ultrasound surveillance and Doppler until delivery
options.rationale: Quintero I TTTS without treatment progresses in 30 percent of cases with poor outcomes; expectant management is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer maternal indomethacin to reduce polyhydramnios in the recipient sac as a temporising measure
options.rationale: Maternal indomethacin causes premature ductal closure and is not part of standard TTTS management. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Urgent referral to a fetal therapy centre for fetoscopic laser ablation as first-line treatment
options.rationale: Quintero stage I TTTS in MCDA twins at 19 weeks (DVP under 2 cm donor and over 8 cm recipient) is treated with fetoscopic laser photocoagulation, the only therapy shown to improve dual survival. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Schedule serial amnioreduction of the polyhydramniotic sac every two weeks until 26 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Amnioreduction provides only symptomatic relief and is inferior to laser for dual survival; reserved for late-presenting or post-laser cases. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Selective fetal reduction of the donor twin by intracardiac potassium chloride injection now
options.rationale: Selective reduction is reserved for very severe disease with one moribund twin or significant anomaly; not appropriate for Quintero stage I. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: TTTS complicates around 15 percent of MCDA pregnancies and is diagnosed by DVP under 2 cm in donor and DVP over 8 cm in recipient before 20 weeks (or over 10 cm at 20 to 26 weeks). Quintero staging from I (oligo + poly, both bladders seen) through V (death of one twin) guides counselling. Fetoscopic laser photocoagulation of placental anastomoses is first-line for stages II to IV between 16 and 26 weeks, with dual survival around 65 percent. Without treatment, perinatal mortality is up to 90 percent. MCDA twins should be scanned every 2 weeks from 16 weeks.
key_points: MCDA twins: 2-weekly USS from 16 weeks.
TTTS: DVP under 2 cm donor + over 8 cm recipient.
Quintero stages I to V.
Fetoscopic laser is first-line for stages II to IV.
Dual survival around 65 percent post-laser.
explanation.pearls: Twin anaemia-polycythaemia sequence (TAPS) is the chronic counterpart of TTTS without amniotic fluid discordance - diagnose with middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity discordance (donor MCA PSV over 1.5 MoM, recipient under 0.8 MoM).

subtopic_detail: Cervical insufficiency with funnelling in second trimester
stem: A 31-year-old G3P0 woman at 19 weeks gestation presents with painless pelvic pressure for 24 hours. Her two previous pregnancies ended at 22 and 23 weeks with painless cervical dilatation. Today transvaginal ultrasound shows a closed external os with cervical length 8 mm and funnelling halfway down the canal. Membranes are intact. There is no bleeding or signs of chorioamnionitis.

data_table.Examination: Soft uterus, no tenderness, no bleeding, closed external os on speculum
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: CL 8 mm, T-shape funnelling; FBC normal; CRP 5; high vaginal swab pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence weekly intramuscular 17-hydroxyprogesterone caproate alone without cervical intervention
options.rationale: Progesterone alone is appropriate for short cervix without prior preterm birth, but rescue cerclage adds benefit in this high-risk history; progesterone alone is insufficient here. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Discharge home with antibiotics and review with repeat transvaginal scan in two weeks time
options.rationale: Delaying intervention risks rapid further dilatation and membrane rupture; this patient meets criteria for rescue cerclage now. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Insert pessary today as the primary intervention to support the cervix mechanically until viability
options.rationale: Pessary use in singleton cervical insufficiency has not consistently reduced preterm birth in trials; cerclage is the evidence-based intervention here. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Strict bed rest until 24 weeks gestation with weekly cervical length surveillance ultrasounds
options.rationale: Bed rest has not been shown to prolong pregnancy and increases thromboembolic risk; not an effective stand-alone intervention. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Insert McDonald cerclage today after multidisciplinary counselling and exclusion of infection
options.rationale: Two prior mid-trimester losses with short cervix on TVUS support rescue cerclage; with no current infection or membrane rupture, McDonald cerclage offers prolongation of pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency is suspected when there is painless mid-trimester loss/preterm birth, especially recurrent. Indication-based cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks is offered after three or more mid-trimester losses. Ultrasound-indicated cerclage at 16 to 24 weeks is offered when prior mid-trimester loss/preterm birth and cervical length less than 25 mm. Rescue cerclage may be offered for cervical dilatation before 26 weeks without infection or membrane rupture. Vaginal progesterone is added if cervical length is less than 25 mm in absence of prior preterm birth history.
key_points: History-indicated cerclage: 3+ mid-trimester losses, place 12-14 weeks.
USS-indicated cerclage: prior PTB + CL under 25 mm at 16-24 weeks.
Rescue cerclage: dilated cervix without infection or PROM under 26 weeks.
Vaginal progesterone: CL under 25 mm without prior PTB.
Exclude infection before any cerclage placement.
explanation.pearls: Bulging membranes can be reduced with steep Trendelenburg and bladder filling before McDonald suture placement - a 16-gauge urinary catheter draining and clamping with 500 mL saline often lifts membranes high enough to allow safe suture passage.

subtopic_detail: Refractory atonic postpartum haemorrhage second-line uterotonic
stem: A 28-year-old G2P2 woman has delivered a 3.9 kg boy vaginally 25 minutes ago after an oxytocin-augmented labour. Estimated blood loss is now 1500 mL with ongoing brisk bleeding from an atonic uterus that softens between bimanual compression. She has received oxytocin 10 IU IM, then 40 IU in 1000 mL crystalloid infusion, IV ergometrine 250 mcg and tranexamic acid 1 g. Her BP is falling and uterus remains soft. She has no history of asthma or significant hypertension.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/52, HR 124, T 36.6, SpO2 98 percent on 6 L oxygen via mask
data_table.Examination: Uterus soft on bimanual compression, no genital tract lacerations on inspection
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L on point-of-care; 4 units RBC + FFP ordered
data_table.Medications: Already received oxytocin, ergometrine and tranexamic acid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next uterotonic step?

options.text: Insert intrauterine balloon tamponade as the next step without further pharmacological therapy
options.rationale: Balloon tamponade is appropriate after maximal pharmacological therapy has failed, not before completing the uterotonic ladder; carboprost should be tried first. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

options.text: Repeat intravenous ergometrine 250 mcg now, then reassess uterine tone in five minutes
options.rationale: Repeat ergometrine adds little after one failed dose and risks severe hypertension; the next pharmacological step is carboprost. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

options.text: Sublingual misoprostol 800 mcg as the priority next-line uterotonic over other agents
options.rationale: Misoprostol is acceptable when carboprost is contraindicated or unavailable, but carboprost is preferred next-line in this asthma-free patient. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

options.text: Intramuscular carboprost 250 mcg every 15 minutes up to a maximum of 8 doses total
options.rationale: Carboprost is the next-line uterotonic after oxytocin and ergometrine have failed in atonic PPH; absence of asthma history makes it appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

options.text: Repeat intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g now while waiting for blood products to arrive
options.rationale: A second dose of tranexamic acid is reasonable if bleeding continues 30 minutes after the first, but does not address ongoing uterine atony; carboprost is needed. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

explanation.summary: Atonic PPH accounts for around 70 percent of major obstetric haemorrhage. Stepwise medical management: oxytocin 10 IU IM/IV then 40 IU infusion, ergometrine 250 to 500 mcg IM or slow IV (avoid if HTN, pre-eclampsia or cardiovascular disease), carboprost 250 mcg IM every 15 minutes up to 8 doses (avoid in asthma), misoprostol 800 mcg sublingual or rectal, and IV tranexamic acid 1 g (repeatable once at 30 minutes). Mechanical measures include bimanual compression, intrauterine balloon tamponade, B-lynch suture and uterine artery ligation; hysterectomy is the last resort.
key_points: Oxytocin first-line; ergometrine second except in HTN.
Carboprost third-line; avoid in asthma.
Misoprostol useful alternative.
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV, repeatable at 30 minutes.
Escalate to balloon, B-lynch, ligation, hysterectomy if persistent.
explanation.pearls: Recombinant factor VIIa is reserved for major obstetric haemorrhage with normalised acidosis, calcium and platelets - giving it earlier into a coagulopathic patient does not work and risks thromboembolism.

sources.label: WOMAN Trial Tranexamic Acid in PPH

subtopic_detail: Pertussis in young infant antibiotic choice
stem: A 5-week-old previously well girl presents with a 7-day history of coryza followed by paroxysms of coughing ending in cyanotic spells and post-tussive vomiting. She has had no inspiratory whoop. Her older brother had a persistent cough for several weeks. She has had no immunisations yet as she is approaching her 6-week vaccinations. She is feeding poorly between paroxysms.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 48, SpO2 92 percent room air during paroxysm, T 36.9, weight 4.2 kg
data_table.Examination: Conjunctival haemorrhages, mild subcostal recession, otherwise unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 26 with lymphocytosis 18; pertussis PCR sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic treatment?

options.text: Inhaled salbutamol via spacer every 4 hours and oral prednisolone for 3 days to reduce inflammation
options.rationale: Bronchodilators and steroids do not modify pertussis cough paroxysms; treatment is supportive plus a macrolide. (Source: RCH CPG Pertussis)

options.text: Discharge home with reassurance as antibiotics do not alter the clinical course of pertussis
options.rationale: Antibiotics started in catarrhal or early paroxysmal phase shorten infectivity and may attenuate symptoms; infants under 6 months require admission regardless of treatment for apnoea monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG Pertussis)

options.text: Admit and commence oral azithromycin 10 mg per kg daily for 5 days with cardiorespiratory monitoring
options.rationale: Azithromycin is first-line for pertussis in infants under 1 month and over (10 mg/kg/day for 5 days); admission is mandatory under 6 months due to apnoea risk. (Source: RCH CPG Pertussis)

options.text: Oral erythromycin 12.5 mg per kg every 6 hours for 14 days under outpatient daily review
options.rationale: Erythromycin in neonates carries increased risk of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis; azithromycin is preferred under 1 month. (Source: RCH CPG Pertussis)

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg per kg every 6 hours as empirical lower respiratory cover
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin has no useful activity against Bordetella pertussis; macrolide therapy is required. (Source: RCH CPG Pertussis)

explanation.summary: Pertussis classically progresses through catarrhal (1 to 2 weeks), paroxysmal (2 to 8 weeks) and convalescent phases. Infants under 6 months are at risk of apnoea, pneumonia, seizures and death. Azithromycin (10 mg/kg/day for 5 days) is first-line under 1 month; clarithromycin or azithromycin from 1 month onward. Public health notification is mandatory. Postexposure prophylaxis for high-risk contacts (pregnancy 3rd trimester, household contacts of infant under 6 months) with the same macrolide. Promote maternal pertussis vaccination during each pregnancy from 20 weeks to prevent neonatal infection.
key_points: Infant under 6 months - admit for apnoea monitoring.
Azithromycin 10 mg/kg/day for 5 days under 1 month.
Erythromycin in neonates linked to pyloric stenosis.
Notifiable disease in Australia.
Maternal vaccination each pregnancy from 20 weeks.
explanation.pearls: Lymphocytosis greater than 50 percent of WCC in young infants with pertussis is associated with pulmonary hypertension and mortality - exchange transfusion or ECMO may be life-saving in severe cases.

subtopic_detail: Threadworm infection in primary-school child
stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with 2 weeks of nocturnal perianal itching that wakes him and his 9-year-old sister from sleep. His mother has seen small white moving threads at the anus in the early morning. The household includes both parents and the two children, all otherwise well with no abdominal pain or weight loss.

data_table.Examination: Perianal excoriation, no bleeding, abdominal examination unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Sticky-tape test confirmed Enterobius eggs

lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment plan?

options.text: Hygiene measures alone without medication including hand washing, short nails and washed linen
options.rationale: Hygiene alone rarely clears established infection within reasonable time; medication is needed to interrupt the cycle. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Threadworm)

options.text: Single dose ivermectin to the index child only with second dose given two weeks later in clinic
options.rationale: Ivermectin is not first-line for threadworm; mebendazole to all household members is preferred. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Threadworm)

options.text: Three-day course of albendazole 400 mg daily to all household members in a single course
options.rationale: Mebendazole is the first-line agent in Australia; albendazole is an alternative when mebendazole is unavailable or contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Threadworm)

options.text: Single dose pyrantel pamoate 10 mg per kg to the index child only with hygiene measures alone
options.rationale: Treating only the index child leaves household reservoir; reinfection is highly likely without whole-household treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Threadworm)

options.text: Single dose mebendazole 100 mg to all household members and repeat the same dose in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Therapeutic guidelines recommend treating the entire household with single-dose mebendazole 100 mg repeated in 2 weeks to break the autoinfection cycle. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Threadworm)

explanation.summary: Enterobius vermicularis (threadworm) is common in young children and spreads through household autoinfection. Classic presentation is perianal itch waking the child. Diagnosis is clinical or confirmed by sticky-tape test taken in the early morning. Australian first-line treatment is mebendazole 100 mg as single dose, repeated in 2 weeks, given to all household members regardless of symptoms. Adjuncts include hand hygiene, short fingernails, daily morning showers and hot washing of bed linen and night clothing for the 2 days after each treatment.
key_points: Treat entire household to break cycle.
Mebendazole 100 mg single dose then repeat at 2 weeks.
Hygiene: hand wash, short nails, morning shower, hot wash bedding.
Albendazole is alternative if mebendazole unavailable.
Reassure school - no need to exclude after treatment.
explanation.pearls: Mebendazole is not licensed in Australia for children under 6 months and has limited safety data under 1 year - in infants, consult Therapeutic Guidelines and consider pyrantel pamoate or topical petrolatum as alternatives.

subtopic_detail: Primary monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis first-line management
stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought in because he has never had a consistent dry night. He wets 5 to 7 nights per week and has had occasional brief dry spells of up to 1 week. He has no daytime urinary symptoms, no constipation, normal growth, and develops typically. His father had nocturnal enuresis until age 9. The child finds the wetting upsetting and his mother asks about treatment options.

data_table.Examination: Normal external genitalia, abdomen soft non-tender, normal lower limb neurology
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis negative for glucose, protein and infection

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Intranasal desmopressin 20 mcg at bedtime with strict fluid restriction from 2 hours before sleep
options.rationale: Intranasal desmopressin is no longer recommended in Australia in children due to hyponatraemic seizures; oral melt is used when desmopressin is required, after alarm therapy. (Source: RCH CPG Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Enuresis alarm therapy with parental supervision for a minimum of 8 weeks
options.rationale: Enuresis alarm is first-line for motivated children over 5 with primary monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis; lasting cure rates around 60 percent after 8 to 12 weeks of consistent use. (Source: RCH CPG Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Continue toilet training reminders and reward chart only without alarm or pharmacological therapy
options.rationale: Reward charts alone have low success in established nocturnal enuresis; alarm therapy is superior when family-engaged treatment is sought. (Source: RCH CPG Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Imipramine 25 mg at bedtime as long-term pharmacological treatment for nightly wetting
options.rationale: Tricyclics are reserved for refractory cases due to overdose mortality and cardiotoxicity; not first-line. (Source: RCH CPG Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Referral for urodynamic studies and renal tract ultrasound before considering any treatment
options.rationale: Imaging and urodynamics are not indicated in primary monosymptomatic enuresis with normal examination; reserved for daytime symptoms, recurrent UTIs or abnormal findings. (Source: RCH CPG Nocturnal Enuresis)

explanation.summary: Primary nocturnal enuresis is involuntary wetting during sleep without a 6-month dry period, with no daytime symptoms. Treatment is offered from age 6 when child and family are motivated. Alarm therapy is first-line with 60 percent lasting cure rate after 8 to 12 weeks; relapses respond to a further course. Oral desmopressin is second-line for older children or short-term cover (school camps). Reward charts may complement alarm therapy. Look for constipation, daytime symptoms, snoring/OSA, behavioural concerns and diabetes if atypical features present.
key_points: Treat from age 6 with motivated family.
Alarm therapy first-line; 8 to 12 weeks minimum.
Oral desmopressin melt second-line.
Tricyclics reserved for refractory cases.
Screen for constipation, daytime symptoms, OSA, diabetes.
explanation.pearls: Desmopressin tablets/melt should be taken without fluids around the dose - children given desmopressin who drink large volumes overnight are at risk of severe hyponatraemic seizures, which is why the intranasal preparation was withdrawn.

subtopic_detail: Allergic rhinitis with sleep disturbance treatment escalation
stem: A 9-year-old girl with seasonal allergic rhinitis presents in spring with persistent rhinorrhoea, nasal blockage and itchy eyes. She wakes most nights with mouth breathing and is excessively tired at school. She has been using oral cetirizine 10 mg daily for 3 weeks without much improvement and has no other medical history.

data_table.Examination: Pale boggy nasal mucosa, conjunctival injection, no postnasal cobblestoning, tonsils 2+ symmetric
data_table.Investigations: Not required for diagnosis
data_table.Medications: Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily for 3 weeks

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Refer for sublingual immunotherapy as the next step before intensifying medical therapy further
options.rationale: Allergen immunotherapy is considered after maximal pharmacological therapy and confirmed allergen sensitisation; not the immediate next step. (Source: ASCIA Allergic Rhinitis)

options.text: Switch cetirizine to a sedating antihistamine such as promethazine for nightly use only
options.rationale: Sedating antihistamines impair learning and worsen daytime fatigue; non-sedating agents are preferred and addition of intranasal steroid is the priority. (Source: ASCIA Allergic Rhinitis)

options.text: Add oral montelukast 5 mg nightly to the existing cetirizine for a 3-month treatment trial
options.rationale: Montelukast has modest efficacy and a TGA boxed warning for neuropsychiatric effects; reserved for cases unresponsive to topical corticosteroids. (Source: ASCIA Allergic Rhinitis)

options.text: Add regular intranasal corticosteroid spray to the cetirizine for at least 4 weeks
