options.rationale: Intranasal corticosteroid is the most effective single agent for persistent allergic rhinitis and is recommended as add-on or first-line for moderate-severe disease in Australian guidance. (Source: ASCIA Allergic Rhinitis)

options.text: Commence a 5-day course of oral prednisolone 30 mg daily to break the cycle of symptoms
options.rationale: Oral steroids are reserved for severe refractory cases; routine use for moderate allergic rhinitis is inappropriate due to systemic side effects. (Source: ASCIA Allergic Rhinitis)

explanation.summary: Allergic rhinitis is classified by ARIA as intermittent or persistent and mild or moderate-severe. Mild intermittent responds to non-sedating oral antihistamines or saline rinses; moderate-severe persistent (sleep affected, school/work impact) requires regular intranasal corticosteroid as cornerstone therapy. Combination intranasal corticosteroid + intranasal antihistamine sprays improve speed of onset. Saline rinse before sprays improves drug delivery. Allergen immunotherapy is offered if pharmacotherapy fails or to modify long-term disease progression.
key_points: Moderate-severe persistent - regular intranasal corticosteroid.
Mild intermittent - non-sedating antihistamine or saline.
Combine antihistamine + steroid if single agent fails.
Avoid sedating antihistamines and oral steroids.
Immunotherapy after maximal pharmacotherapy failure.
explanation.pearls: Intranasal corticosteroid sprays work best when angled outward toward the lateral wall, not toward the septum - misdirected delivery causes septal irritation and epistaxis without improving symptoms.

subtopic_detail: Uncomplicated infant gastro-oesophageal reflux counselling
stem: A 4-month-old breastfed boy presents with 6 weeks of frequent effortless regurgitation after most feeds. He gains weight along the 50th centile, feeds eagerly, and is otherwise well. There is no projectile vomiting, no blood, no bile, no back-arching during feeds and no respiratory symptoms. His mother is exhausted by laundry and is asking whether medication will help.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 36, T 36.7, weight 7.2 kg (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Alert, smiling, soft abdomen, no abdominal distension, hydrated
data_table.Investigations: None required

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Switch from breast to extensively hydrolysed formula and review the infant in 2 weeks time
options.rationale: Formula switch is reserved for suspected cows-milk protein allergy with red flags; not indicated in an exclusively breastfed thriving infant with no allergy features. (Source: RCH CPG Gastro-oesophageal Reflux)

options.text: Commence oral omeprazole 1 mg per kg once daily for an 8-week empirical treatment trial
options.rationale: PPIs are ineffective for uncomplicated reflux without oesophagitis and increase infection and fracture risk; not indicated in a well-thriving baby. (Source: RCH CPG Gastro-oesophageal Reflux)

options.text: Commence regular oral ranitidine 4 mg per kg twice daily as antacid for symptom control in this infant
options.rationale: Ranitidine has been withdrawn due to NDMA contamination; H2 blockers are no longer routine for uncomplicated reflux. (Source: RCH CPG Gastro-oesophageal Reflux)

options.text: Refer to paediatric gastroenterology for upper endoscopy and pH study before any conservative measures
options.rationale: Invasive investigation is reserved for red flags (poor growth, haematemesis, persistent respiratory or neurological symptoms); not for uncomplicated regurgitation. (Source: RCH CPG Gastro-oesophageal Reflux)

options.text: Parental reassurance and conservative measures including upright positioning after feeds
options.rationale: Uncomplicated infant gastro-oesophageal reflux is physiological and self-resolves by 12 to 14 months; reassurance and feeding-position advice are first-line. (Source: RCH CPG Gastro-oesophageal Reflux)

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated infant gastro-oesophageal reflux is regurgitation without functional concern; it affects up to 40 percent of healthy infants and resolves spontaneously by 12 to 14 months. Reflux disease (GORD) involves complications: faltering growth, recurrent respiratory symptoms, oesophagitis, food refusal, distressed behaviour. Management is stepwise from parental reassurance, upright positioning, smaller more frequent feeds, thickened formula (not breastfed infants) through to acid suppression only if GORD is confirmed. Red flags (bilious vomiting, blood, bulging fontanelle, abdominal distension) demand urgent assessment for alternative diagnoses.
key_points: Uncomplicated reflux: reassurance, position upright after feeds.
PPIs and H2 blockers offer no benefit without GORD.
Thickened feeds OK for formula-fed infants.
GORD red flags: weight loss, recurrent wheeze, oesophagitis.
Bilious vomit, blood, bulging fontanelle - urgent assessment.
explanation.pearls: Sandifer syndrome (dystonic head/neck posturing during feeds) is a manifestation of GORD that mimics seizures - oesophageal pH/impedance monitoring rather than EEG is the appropriate first investigation.

subtopic_detail: Acute paediatric tonsillopharyngitis antibiotic decision
stem: A 9-year-old girl living in a remote Northern Territory community presents with a 24-hour sore throat, fever to 39.2 degrees Celsius and difficulty swallowing solids. She has tender anterior cervical lymph nodes, large exudative tonsils and no cough. Her family has a history of acute rheumatic fever in an older sibling. She has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral exudative tonsils, tender anterior cervical nodes, no rash, no cough
data_table.Investigations: Throat swab taken for GAS culture

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic management while awaiting throat swab results?

options.text: Single dose oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 500 mg now followed by symptomatic care alone
options.rationale: A single dose of phenoxymethylpenicillin is inadequate; either a 10-day oral course or single IM benzathine dose is required for streptococcal eradication. (Source: RHDAustralia Guidelines)

options.text: Oral azithromycin 12 mg per kg daily for 5 days while awaiting the throat culture result
options.rationale: Macrolides are reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy; resistance is rising and they are not first-line in this scenario. (Source: RHDAustralia Guidelines)

options.text: Single intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin 900 mg now with single follow-up review
options.rationale: Australian guidelines recommend single-dose IM benzathine benzylpenicillin for sore throat in children at high risk of acute rheumatic fever (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander background, remote community, family history). (Source: RHDAustralia Guidelines)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg per kg twice daily for 10 days starting today after sample collection
options.rationale: Oral amoxicillin 50 mg/kg daily for 10 days is an alternative if IM benzathine penicillin is unavailable; adherence over 10 days is the challenge in remote settings, hence IM preferred. (Source: RHDAustralia Guidelines)

options.text: Watchful waiting with paracetamol for 48 hours before reconsidering antibiotic prescription
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is appropriate in low-risk populations but not for high-ARF-risk children where empirical antibiotics are recommended to prevent rheumatic fever. (Source: RHDAustralia Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute sore throat management in Australia is stratified by ARF risk. High-risk groups include Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people, especially in northern Australia, those with personal or family history of ARF and people living in crowded conditions. In high-risk groups, all sore throats with GAS pharyngitis features are treated empirically. Single-dose IM benzathine benzylpenicillin is preferred for adherence; oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 500 mg BD for 10 days is an alternative. Low-risk populations may use Centor or FeverPAIN criteria with delayed prescription.
key_points: Risk-stratify by ARF risk.
High-risk: empirical IM benzathine penicillin G.
Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 10 days is alternative.
Macrolides for severe penicillin allergy.
Low-risk: Centor or FeverPAIN-guided delayed prescription.
explanation.pearls: Persistent throat carriage of group A streptococcus after appropriate treatment in a low-risk patient rarely needs eradication - it does not progress to invasive disease and adds antibiotic exposure without benefit.

subtopic_detail: Tetralogy of Fallot hypercyanotic spell first-line manoeuvre
stem: An 8-month-old boy known to have unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot is brought to the emergency department after a 10-minute episode of inconsolable crying, then deep cyanosis. He is now distressed, deeply blue and his mother says his murmur has become quieter. Cardiac surgery is planned in 2 weeks. He has no fever and no recent infections.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 56, SpO2 62 percent on room air, T 36.9
data_table.Examination: Marked central cyanosis, soft systolic murmur (previously harsh), no respiratory distress signs out of proportion
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate first manoeuvre?

options.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline 10 mcg per kg to support cardiac output during the spell
options.rationale: Adrenaline increases right ventricular outflow tract spasm by beta-agonism and worsens the spell; beta-blockade is the relevant pharmacology. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: Begin chest compressions and bag-mask ventilation as the first response to the deep cyanosis present
options.rationale: The child is not in cardiac arrest; aggressive resuscitation without addressing the shunt physiology will not resolve the spell. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: Commence inhaled nitric oxide via face mask to dilate the pulmonary vasculature immediately now
options.rationale: Pulmonary vasodilation is not the mechanism in tet spells; the problem is right ventricular outflow tract obstruction with shunt reversal. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: Provide intravenous frusemide 1 mg per kg to reduce preload and lung congestion in this child
options.rationale: Tet spells are not driven by pulmonary oedema; diuresis reduces preload and worsens the shunt. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: Place the child in the knee-to-chest position and give high-flow oxygen via face mask
options.rationale: Knee-to-chest increases systemic vascular resistance, reverses right-to-left shunting and is the immediate first step in a tet spell, with simultaneous high-flow oxygen. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic Heart Disease)

explanation.summary: A tet spell results from acute increase in right ventricular outflow tract obstruction combined with falling systemic vascular resistance, causing right-to-left shunting and profound hypoxia. Immediate management is to settle the child in knee-to-chest (or parental cuddling with knees flexed) plus high-flow oxygen. Pharmacological escalation includes morphine 0.1 mg/kg IM or IV to abolish hyperpnoea, IV phenylephrine to raise SVR, IV propranolol to relax the RVOT, sodium bicarbonate for acidosis and prompt referral for surgical or palliative shunting if recurrent. Quiet murmur indicates near-total RVOT obstruction.
key_points: Knee-to-chest + high-flow O2 are immediate first steps.
Morphine 0.1 mg/kg blunts hyperpnoea.
Phenylephrine raises SVR and reduces shunt.
Propranolol relaxes RVOT muscular obstruction.
Quieter murmur means worse spell.
explanation.pearls: Beta-blockade with propranolol is used both acutely (intravenously) and as outpatient prophylaxis (oral 1 to 2 mg/kg/day) in babies awaiting repair - schedule surgical repair before 6 months when spells start to recur to avoid long-term right ventricular damage.

subtopic_detail: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia salt-wasting crisis in newborn
stem: A 12-day-old breastfed boy is brought in by his parents after 3 days of poor feeding, lethargy and weight loss. He was born at term following an uneventful pregnancy. Examination shows hyperpigmented scrotal skin, weight 8 percent below birth weight, and obvious dehydration. Newborn screening for CAH was sent 9 days ago and result is pending.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 56, BP 64/38, T 36.4, SpO2 96 percent
data_table.Examination: Sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, cool peripheries, normal male external genitalia with hyperpigmentation
data_table.Investigations: Na 121 mmol/L, K 6.9 mmol/L, glucose 2.4 mmol/L, urea 8.4, creatinine 78, VBG pH 7.28

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate resuscitation step?

options.text: Withhold steroid until ACTH and 17-hydroxyprogesterone results return to confirm CAH diagnosis
options.rationale: Withholding steroid in a shocked baby with the classic biochemistry of salt-wasting CAH risks death; take samples and treat immediately. (Source: RCH CPG Adrenal Crisis)

options.text: Oral fludrocortisone 50 mcg now and admit for observation pending the newborn screening result
options.rationale: Oral therapy is inadequate for a shocked vomiting infant; intravenous hydrocortisone is required and fludrocortisone is started later when stable. (Source: RCH CPG Adrenal Crisis)

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 0.5 mL per kg of 10 percent solution to stabilise the cardiac membrane
options.rationale: Calcium is indicated when hyperkalaemia produces ECG changes; the immediate priority is steroid replacement plus volume resuscitation, which itself corrects the potassium. (Source: RCH CPG Adrenal Crisis)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 25 mg stat plus 0.9 percent saline with 5 percent dextrose 20 mL per kg bolus
options.rationale: Acute adrenal crisis in suspected salt-wasting CAH requires immediate parenteral hydrocortisone (stress dose) and isotonic dextrose-containing fluid to correct shock, hyponatraemia and hypoglycaemia simultaneously. (Source: RCH CPG Adrenal Crisis)

options.text: Intravenous 3 percent saline 5 mL per kg over 1 hour to rapidly correct the severe hyponatraemia
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is reserved for symptomatic severe hyponatraemia with seizures; rapid correction here risks osmotic demyelination and does not treat the underlying adrenal crisis. (Source: RCH CPG Adrenal Crisis)

explanation.summary: Classical salt-wasting CAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency, most common) presents in the second week of life with vomiting, weight loss, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia and shock. Males may not have ambiguous genitalia, making missed diagnosis at delivery common before newborn screen returns. Acute management is IV hydrocortisone 25 mg stat (infant) plus IV 0.9 percent saline with 5 percent dextrose 20 mL/kg bolus, then stress-dose hydrocortisone infusion. Once stable, transition to oral hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone with sodium chloride supplementation. Genitogram, karyotype, ACTH, 17-OHP and renin sent on presentation.
key_points: Second week of life vomiting + weight loss - think CAH.
Biochemistry: low Na, high K, low glucose, metabolic acidosis.
IV hydrocortisone 25 mg stat + saline-dextrose bolus.
Fludrocortisone and salt added once stable.
Send 17-OHP, ACTH, renin BEFORE first steroid dose.
explanation.pearls: Sick-day rules: triple the maintenance hydrocortisone dose during fever or vomiting and give IM hydrocortisone if oral dose cannot be retained - parents should be supplied with an emergency injection kit at diagnosis.

subtopic_detail: Down syndrome newborn cardiac screening pathway
stem: A term newborn male has clinical features of trisomy 21 on day 1: hypotonia, upslanting palpebral fissures, single transverse palmar creases bilaterally and a flat nasal bridge. He is feeding well and pulse oximetry congenital heart disease screen is normal. His chromosomal microarray result is pending and the parents ask about cardiac assessment.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 44, SpO2 99 percent right hand and right foot, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: Soft systolic murmur audible at left sternal edge; femoral pulses palpable equally bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: Pulse oximetry CHD screen normal; chromosomal microarray pending
data_table.Medications: Vitamin K and hepatitis B vaccine given at birth

lead_in: What is the most appropriate cardiac investigation plan?

options.text: Chest radiograph and 12-lead electrocardiograph in the first month with cardiology review later
options.rationale: CXR and ECG cannot exclude septal defects; echocardiography is required. (Source: RCH CPG Down Syndrome)

options.text: Formal transthoracic echocardiogram performed within the first 4 to 6 weeks of life
options.rationale: Around 45 percent of infants with Down syndrome have congenital heart disease and pulse oximetry screen misses non-cyanotic lesions like AVSD and VSD; transthoracic echocardiogram is recommended in the first weeks of life. (Source: RCH CPG Down Syndrome)

options.text: Pulse oximetry CHD screen alone with no further imaging while infant remains asymptomatic clinically
options.rationale: Pulse oximetry misses non-cyanotic CHD common in Down syndrome (AVSD, VSD, PDA); formal echocardiography is required. (Source: RCH CPG Down Syndrome)

options.text: Cardiac MRI under general anaesthetic as the screening investigation of choice within 6 weeks
options.rationale: MRI is reserved for select complex lesions; echocardiography is the first-line non-invasive screen for CHD in Down syndrome. (Source: RCH CPG Down Syndrome)

options.text: Defer cardiac imaging until the murmur is louder or symptoms emerge later in infancy
options.rationale: Awaiting symptoms risks irreversible pulmonary vascular disease in undiagnosed AVSD; early echocardiography is mandatory in trisomy 21. (Source: RCH CPG Down Syndrome)

explanation.summary: Down syndrome is associated with congenital heart disease in about 45 percent of children, atrioventricular septal defect being the most common, followed by VSD, ASD and PDA. Early echocardiography in the first 4 to 6 weeks identifies lesions before pulmonary vascular disease develops; surgical repair of AVSD is typically performed by 6 months. Other screening on the Down syndrome health check schedule includes hearing assessment at birth and 6 months, ophthalmology at 6 to 12 months, thyroid function annually, coeliac screen, atlantoaxial assessment before contact sport, sleep study if obstructive symptoms, and developmental surveillance.
key_points: About 45 percent CHD - AVSD most common.
Echocardiogram at 4 to 6 weeks regardless of pulse oximetry screen.
AVSD repair typically before 6 months.
Thyroid screening annually.
Atlantoaxial assessment before contact sports.
explanation.pearls: Atrioventricular septal defects in Down syndrome have early-onset pulmonary vascular disease compared with non-Down AVSD - elective repair is brought forward to 3 to 6 months to avoid Eisenmenger physiology.

sources.label: Down Syndrome Australia Health Care Guide

subtopic_detail: Persistent neonatal anuria suggesting posterior urethral valves
stem: A 3-day-old term male newborn has not passed urine since hospital discharge 24 hours after delivery and now appears irritable. His abdomen is mildly distended with a palpable bladder. Antenatal ultrasound at 32 weeks reported bilateral hydronephrosis but the parents declined further follow-up scans. His mother is otherwise well and birth was uncomplicated.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158, RR 52, T 36.7, weight 3.2 kg (down 9 percent from birth weight)
data_table.Examination: Palpable bladder to umbilicus, no abdominal mass, normal external genitalia, dribbling urine stream when expressed
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 168 micromol/L, Na 132, K 6.4, bedside USS distended bladder, bilateral hydronephrosis, dilated posterior urethra

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Suprapubic aspirate of urine for culture and discharge home with outpatient nephrology review
options.rationale: Aspiration alone does not relieve bladder outlet obstruction; ongoing obstruction continues to damage the kidneys and bladder. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

options.text: Fluid challenge with normal saline 20 mL per kg then reassess for spontaneous voiding within 4 hours
options.rationale: Volume loading without relieving obstruction risks bladder injury and worsening hydronephrosis; catheter decompression is required first. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 1 mg per kg to promote urine output before any imaging is performed
options.rationale: Frusemide does not relieve mechanical obstruction; outflow must be established first by catheterisation, and dehydration may worsen the picture. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

options.text: Urgent micturating cystourethrogram first before any other intervention to confirm the diagnosis
options.rationale: MCUG is required to confirm the diagnosis but only after the bladder is decompressed and the baby is stabilised; not the first step in an obstructed septic-risk neonate. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

options.text: Insert a small-bore urethral catheter and admit to neonatal unit with urology referral
options.rationale: Suspected posterior urethral valves are decompressed urgently with bladder catheterisation, then transferred for paediatric urological assessment and surgical valve ablation. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

explanation.summary: Posterior urethral valves are the commonest cause of severe lower urinary tract obstruction in male infants. Antenatal findings include bilateral hydronephrosis, dilated bladder and keyhole sign of dilated posterior urethra. Postnatally, suspect in any boy with weak stream, palpable bladder or anuria. Immediate management is catheter decompression with a small feeding tube (urethral catheter balloons can lodge in dilated posterior urethra), correction of electrolytes and prophylactic antibiotics. Definitive surgery is cystoscopic valve ablation. Long-term complications include chronic kidney disease, bladder dysfunction and need for transplantation in around 30 percent.
key_points: Suspect in boy with weak stream + palpable bladder + AKI.
Catheter decompression first - use feeding tube without balloon.
Send creatinine, electrolytes, USS post-decompression.
Cystoscopic valve ablation is definitive.
Long-term CKD risk - lifelong urology and nephrology follow-up.
explanation.pearls: Post-obstructive diuresis is common after relief of bilateral obstruction - replace urine output 1:1 with 0.45 percent saline plus dextrose for 24 to 48 hours to prevent profound electrolyte and volume disturbance.

sources.label: Australian and New Zealand Society for Paediatric Surgery

subtopic_detail: Adult asthma uncontrolled on ICS-LABA step-up
stem: A 42-year-old woman with adult-onset asthma reports daily symptoms requiring her salbutamol 4 times a day, two night-time awakenings per week, and has used three prednisolone courses in the past year. She is fully adherent with budesonide-formoterol 200/6 mcg twice daily plus reliever and has good inhaler technique demonstrated in clinic today. She does not smoke. Spirometry shows FEV1 of 68 percent predicted with 14 percent reversibility post-bronchodilator.

data_table.Examination: Scattered end-expiratory wheeze, no clubbing
data_table.Investigations: FEV1 68 percent predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.65, eosinophils 0.35, IgE 88

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next medication change?

options.text: Add oral montelukast 10 mg daily to current ICS-LABA as third controller for symptom control
options.rationale: Montelukast adds modest benefit and carries TGA boxed warning for neuropsychiatric effects; LAMA is preferred third controller. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Add a long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) inhaler as a third controller to current ICS-LABA
options.rationale: Australian asthma guidance recommends LAMA add-on as the preferred step-up when ICS-LABA at medium dose with good adherence and technique fails to control symptoms, before biologics. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Refer immediately for omalizumab biologic therapy without further inhaled corticosteroid trials given symptoms
options.rationale: Biologics are PBS-funded for severe asthma after optimisation of inhaled therapy including LAMA; she has not yet stepped up to triple therapy. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence maintenance oral prednisolone 10 mg daily until symptoms have resolved completely
options.rationale: Maintenance oral steroids carry significant long-term morbidity and are reserved for severe refractory asthma after biologic options exhausted. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Double the dose of inhaled corticosteroid component within current ICS-LABA combination
options.rationale: Doubling ICS provides only small additional benefit beyond medium dose and increases systemic effects; LAMA add-on is preferred per current guidance. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

explanation.summary: Step-up therapy for adult asthma not controlled on medium-dose ICS-LABA, despite good adherence and technique, is triple therapy with addition of a LAMA (tiotropium 5 mcg daily or once-daily ICS-LABA-LAMA combinations). Biologics are PBS-funded for severe asthma based on phenotype: omalizumab (allergic, IgE-driven), mepolizumab/benralizumab (eosinophilic), dupilumab (eosinophilic or type 2 high). Always reassess adherence, inhaler technique, environmental triggers, smoking, comorbidities (rhinitis, reflux, obesity, OSA, mental health) before escalating.
key_points: Confirm adherence and technique before stepping up.
Step 4-5: ICS-LABA medium dose + LAMA add-on.
Biologics for severe disease, phenotype-driven.
Maintenance oral steroids only after biologics fail.
Treat comorbidities: rhinitis, reflux, OSA, obesity, smoking.
explanation.pearls: Always check for vocal cord dysfunction in adult-onset wheeze responding incompletely to maximal therapy - inspiratory wheeze, sudden onset and offset, and laryngoscopy during attack confirm the diagnosis and reduce unnecessary asthma escalation.
